rubric_en: The photographer spent decades meandering across the country with his camera. What was he looking for?
rubric_zh: 这位摄影师几十年来携相机在全国漫游。 他在寻找什么？

paragraphs.zh: 1991 年孟菲斯（李·弗里德兰德*）

paragraphs.en: Few people have taught us to see America quite like Lee Friedlander. The 91-year-old photographer has been making pictures since the late 1940s, focusing largely on what critics and historians describe as the urban social landscape: all of these little jigsaw scenes of our built environment. He notices the everyday moments that go unseen by most, moments so inconsequential that we probably wouldn’t even bother dismissing them as mundane. Friedlander once distilled his approach into a simple ethos: “I just walk and see something interesting.”
paragraphs.zh: 很少有人能像李·弗里德兰德那样教会我们“看见”美国。 这位 91 岁的摄影师自 20 世纪 40 年代末开始拍摄，主要聚焦评论家和历史学家所说的“城市社会景观”——这些由无数支离片段拼成的建成环境景象。他会捕捉到大多数人看不见的日常瞬间，那些微不足道到我们甚至懒得把它们当作平淡无奇去刻意忽略的时刻。弗里德兰德曾把自己的方法概括为一句朴素的理念： “我只是在走着，看看有什么有趣的东西。”

paragraphs.en: Life Still, a monograph of Friedlander’s work that Aperture published this spring, collects photos from the 1950s to the present, and it’s in Friedlander’s careful placement of pictures side by side that these puzzle pieces begin to depict a meaningful and at times delightful whole. Looking at them is like noticing that the song you’re listening to catches the beat of a passing car, or seeing two strangers walk in symmetry on opposite sides of the street. You study Friedlander’s pairs, recognizing the rhymes across time and space. The only clues that date his wanderings arrive in the shape of a mid-century refrigerator, or a certain hairstyle, or a peeling political bumper sticker.
paragraphs.zh: 《Life Still》是 Aperture 于今年春天出版的一本弗里德兰德作品单行本，收录了从 1950 年代到当下的照片；正是在弗里德兰德把照片小心并置的方式下，这些拼图般的片段才逐渐形成一种有意义且时而令人愉快的整体。观赏这些作品，就像你突然发现正在听的歌曲刚好对上了路过汽车的节拍，或看到两个陌生人从街道两侧以对称的姿态同时走来。你会细看弗里德兰德的并列影像，辨认出跨越时间和空间的对应关系。唯一提示他漫步年代的线索，往往是一台中世纪风格的冰箱、某种发型，或一张剥落的政治汽车贴纸。

paragraphs.en: What Friedlander finds “interesting” is often ironic and devilishly funny. An archway marked ENTRANCE provides entry to no building, but rather to endless sky. You can imagine Friedlander’s glee when he happened upon a sculpture with a busted phallus standing next to a painting of the comic-strip character Wimpy chomping on a slender baguette.
paragraphs.zh: 弗里德兰德眼中所谓“有趣”的东西常常带着讽刺意味，且狡黠得可笑。一个写着“ENTRANCE”的拱门，其实并不通向任何建筑，而是通向无尽的天空。你可以想象弗里德兰德在偶然发现一尊下半身像雕塑，旁边却挂着一幅漫画角色 Wimpy 正在咬着一根细长法棍的画面时的得意与快乐。
肯塔基州，1977年（李·弗里德兰德*）

paragraphs.en: Plastic trinkets and graffiti, television sets and bumper stickers, road signs and wigs—many might describe these items as disposable culture, signs of America’s crass habit of regarding anything as art. What of it? Everywhere in his work, we see reminders that this is a land of artists, even if they don’t see themselves as such. In one picture, he focuses on a figurine of a tattoo artist inking a woman’s breast; in another, a lamp fashioned from a tree sits on a book about “Tramp Art.”
paragraphs.zh: 塑料小摆件和涂鸦、电视机和后贴纸、路牌和假发——很多人可能会把这些东西称作“用后即弃”的文化，是美国那种把任何东西都当成艺术的粗犷习惯的标志。可这又如何呢？在他的作品中到处都能看到提醒：这是一个艺术家的土地，即使这些人并不把自己当作艺术家。在一张照片里，他把镜头对准了一个给女人乳房纹身的纹身师雕像；在另一张里，一盏由树木制成的灯放在一本《流浪者艺术》（Tramp Art）书上。

paragraphs.en: Read: The road dogs of the American West
paragraphs.zh: 阅读：《美国西部的流浪者》

paragraphs.en: There aren’t many people in these pictures, but there is presence. Is anything more intimate than the divot left by the owner of a well-worn armchair? A seemingly simple picture of a shop window reminded me of that John Coltrane quote about starting in the middle of a sentence and moving both ways at once: We see what is in front of Friedlander, only the glass captures what is behind him too, and it’s as though all of history has converged in this moment. These pictures can seem like accidents until you notice the unlikely beauty everywhere: the wondrously mathematical shadow of a chain-link fence, a patch of sky mirrored in a window, a bird in the distance that appears perched on a car door. Every now and then, you glimpse Friedlander’s reflection—these are moments that feel slyly autobiographical. We see his shadow cast against a kitschy display that reads NOT ALL WHO WANDER ARE LOST. I like to imagine that he is smiling throughout, a reminder that the artist is at work. There are still more roads to walk, more things to see.
paragraphs.zh: 这些画面中人物并不多，但却有存在感。比起一把被人长期使用过的扶手椅留下的凹痕，还有更亲密的东西吗？一张看似普通的店铺橱窗照片让我想起约翰·科川的那句话：从一句话中间开始，同时向两个方向读下去。我们看到的是弗里德兰德面前的景象，只有玻璃也捕捉到了他身后的部分，于是似乎所有历史在此刻汇聚。这些照片在刚开始时可能显得像偶然，却在你注意到处处存在的非凡美感后改观：一段铁丝网阴影的奇妙几何感，一块映在窗里的天空碎片，一只远处的鸟儿仿佛停在一扇车门上。偶尔，你会瞥见弗里德兰德的倒影——这些时刻带着几分狡黠的自传意味。我们看到他的身影投在一块俗艳招贴板上，上面写着“NOT ALL WHO WANDER ARE LOST”。我想象他始终带着微笑，这提醒我们，艺术家始终在工作。还有更多的路要走，还有更多东西要看。

paragraphs.en: *Images: Lee Friedlander: Life Still (Aperture, 2026). © 2026 Lee Friedlander. Courtesy of Lee Friedlander; Fraenkel Gallery, San Francisco; and Luhring Augustine, New York.
paragraphs.zh: *图片：Lee Friedlander：《Life Still》（Aperture，2026）。© 2026 Lee Friedlander。由Lee Friedlander、美国旧金山Fraenkel Gallery和纽约Luhring Augustine提供。

paragraphs.en: This article was adapted from Hua Hsu’s introduction to Lee Friedlander: Life Still. It appears in the June 2026 print edition with the headline “A Land of Artists.”
paragraphs.zh: 本文改编自Hua Hsu为《Lee Friedlander: Life Still》所写的导言。它刊登在2026年6月的纸媒版本中，标题为“艺术家之地”。
